flashcards.q: Define motion. Give an example showing it is relative.
flashcards.a: Motion: change in position of an object with time relative to a reference point. Relative example: a passenger in a moving bus sees roadside trees moving backward; a person on the road sees the bus moving — same event, different observers.

flashcards.q: Define distance and displacement. Which is scalar and which is vector?
flashcards.a: Distance: total path length covered — scalar (only magnitude). Displacement: shortest straight-line distance from initial to final position — vector (magnitude + direction). Both have SI unit: metre (m).

flashcards.q: Can displacement be zero when distance is non-zero? Give an example.
flashcards.a: Yes. When an object returns to its starting point: distance ≠ 0 but displacement = 0. Example: one complete round of a circular track — distance = 2πr, displacement = 0.

flashcards.q: When does magnitude of displacement = distance?
flashcards.a: When the object moves in a straight line in ONE direction without reversing. Every step of the path directly adds to the straight-line gap from start to finish.

flashcards.q: What does an odometer measure?
flashcards.a: An odometer measures DISTANCE (total path length), not displacement. It records every metre of path regardless of direction.

flashcards.q: Define uniform and non-uniform motion.
flashcards.a: Uniform motion: equal distances in equal intervals of time (constant speed). Non-uniform motion: unequal distances in equal intervals of time (changing speed).

flashcards.q: Formula and SI unit of speed. Is it scalar or vector?
flashcards.a: Speed = distance / time. SI unit: m s⁻¹. Scalar — only magnitude, no direction. Average speed = total distance / total time.

flashcards.q: Formula and SI unit of velocity. Is it scalar or vector?
flashcards.a: Velocity = displacement / time. SI unit: m s⁻¹. Vector — has both magnitude and direction. Average velocity (uniform acceleration) = (u + v) / 2.

flashcards.q: When is average velocity = 0 even though speed ≠ 0?
flashcards.a: When the object returns to its starting position — displacement = 0. Example: Usha swims 90 m to one end and 90 m back in 60 s → avg speed = 180/60 = 3 m s⁻¹, avg velocity = 0/60 = 0 m s⁻¹.

flashcards.q: Define acceleration. Formula, SI unit, and sign convention.
flashcards.a: Acceleration = rate of change of velocity = (v − u) / t. SI unit: m s⁻². Positive (+a): velocity increasing (in direction of motion). Negative (−a / deceleration): velocity decreasing (opposite to motion).

flashcards.q: Define uniform and non-uniform acceleration.
flashcards.a: Uniform acceleration: velocity changes by EQUAL amounts in equal time intervals. V-t graph = straight line. Non-uniform: velocity changes by UNEQUAL amounts. V-t graph = curve. Example of uniform: freely falling body.

flashcards.q: Slope of distance-time graph = ?
flashcards.a: Slope of distance-time graph = SPEED. Straight line (positive slope) → uniform speed. Curve → non-uniform speed. Horizontal line (zero slope) → at rest.

flashcards.q: Slope of velocity-time graph = ?
flashcards.a: Slope of velocity-time graph = ACCELERATION. Horizontal line → zero acceleration (uniform velocity). Straight inclined line → uniform acceleration. Curve → non-uniform acceleration.

flashcards.q: Area under velocity-time graph = ?
flashcards.a: Area under velocity-time graph = DISTANCE (magnitude of displacement) covered. Rectangle area = v × t = distance for uniform velocity. Triangle + rectangle area = ut + ½at² = distance for uniform acceleration.

flashcards.q: State all 3 equations of motion and their names.
flashcards.a: (1) v = u + at [velocity-time relation]. (2) s = ut + ½at² [position-time relation]. (3) v² = u² + 2as [velocity-position relation]. Valid only for uniform (constant) acceleration along a straight line.

flashcards.q: What are the symbols in equations of motion?
flashcards.a: u = initial velocity (m s⁻¹). v = final velocity (m s⁻¹). a = acceleration (m s⁻²). t = time (s). s = distance covered (m).

flashcards.q: Convert 72 km h⁻¹ to m s⁻¹. What is the conversion formula?
flashcards.a: km h⁻¹ × (5/18) = m s⁻¹. So 72 × 5/18 = 20 m s⁻¹. Reverse: m s⁻¹ × (18/5) = km h⁻¹.

flashcards.q: Starting from rest: what is u? Object stops: what is v?
flashcards.a: Starts from rest: u = 0. Object comes to rest (stops): v = 0. These are the most common initial/final conditions in equation-of-motion problems.

flashcards.q: Example 7.7: car brakes at a = −6 m s⁻², stops in 2 s. Find initial speed and stopping distance.
Define uniform circular motion. Is it accelerated?
flashcards.a: Uniform circular motion: object moves in a circular path at CONSTANT speed but continuously changing direction. YES — it is accelerated because the direction of velocity vector changes at every point, meaning velocity changes.

flashcards.q: Why does direction change mean acceleration in circular motion?
flashcards.a: Velocity is a vector = magnitude + direction. Even if speed (magnitude) is constant, changing direction changes the velocity vector → rate of change of velocity ≠ 0 → acceleration exists (centripetal, directed toward centre).

flashcards.q: Formula for speed in uniform circular motion.
flashcards.a: v = 2πr / t. Where r = radius of circular path, t = time period (time for one complete revolution). Circumference = 2πr is the distance covered in one revolution.

flashcards.q: Activity 7.11 — Stone on thread: what happens when released? What does it prove?
flashcards.a: Stone moves in a straight line TANGENTIAL to the circle at the point of release. Proves: (1) velocity direction in circular motion is always along the tangent. (2) Without the centripetal force (thread tension), the object moves in a straight line — demonstrating Newton's 1st Law.

flashcards.q: Three examples of uniform circular motion.
flashcards.a: Moon orbiting Earth; artificial satellite in circular orbit; cyclist at constant speed on a circular track; athlete rotating before throwing a hammer; Earth revolving around the Sun.

flashcards.q: Two-part journey: same distance at 20 km h⁻¹ and 30 km h⁻¹. Average speed?
flashcards.a: Average speed = 2×20×30 / (20+30) = 1200/50 = 24 km h⁻¹. NOT (20+30)/2 = 25, because equal distances (not equal times) are covered at each speed.

flashcards.q: What does the distance-time graph look like for a stationary object? For non-uniform acceleration?
flashcards.a: Stationary: horizontal straight line (slope = 0 = speed). Non-uniform acceleration: non-linear curve on distance-time graph (increasing slope = increasing speed).

flashcards.q: What is retardation (deceleration)?
flashcards.a: Retardation = negative acceleration — when velocity of an object decreases with time. The acceleration acts in the direction OPPOSITE to motion. Example: applying brakes to a car.

flashcards.q: Stone thrown upward at 5 m s⁻¹, g = 10 m s⁻² downward. Max height and time to reach it?
flashcards.a: At max height: v = 0. v² = u² + 2as → 0 = 25 − 20s → s = 1.25 m. v = u + at → 0 = 5 − 10t → t = 0.5 s.
